British church schisms after 1688

This article is about the split in the Churches of England, Scotland and Ireland over the 1689 legal and religious settlement. For the French church in the French Revolution, see [Civil Constitution of the Clergy § Jurors and non-jurors](/wiki/Civil_Constitution_of_the_Clergy#Jurors_and_non-jurors "Civil Constitution of the Clergy").

The **Nonjuring schism** refers to a split in the [established](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") churches of [England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church") and [Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"), following the deposition and exile of [James II and VII](/wiki/James_II_and_VII "James II and VII") in the 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"). As a condition of office, clergy were required to swear allegiance to the ruling monarch; for various reasons, some refused to take the oath to his successors, [William III and II](/wiki/William_III_and_II "William III and II") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"). These individuals were referred to as *non-jurors* or as *non-juring*, from the Latin verb *iūrāre* or *jūrāre*, meaning "to swear an oath".

In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), an estimated 2% of priests refused to swear allegiance in 1689, including nine [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"). Ordinary clergy were allowed to keep their positions but after efforts to compromise failed, the six surviving bishops were removed in 1691. The schismatic Non-Juror Church was formed in 1693 when Bishop [William Lloyd](/wiki/William_Lloyd_(bishop_of_Norwich) "William Lloyd (bishop of Norwich)") appointed his own bishops. His action was opposed by the majority of English Non-Jurors, who remained within the Church of England and are sometimes referred to as "crypto-Non-Jurors". Never large in numbers, the Non-Juror Church rapidly declined after 1715, although minor congregations remained in existence until the 1770s.

In Scotland, the 1690 religious settlement removed [High Church](/wiki/High_Church "High Church") practices and Episcopal bishops, and restored a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity")-structured [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), popularly known as the Kirk. Those [ministers](/wiki/Minister_(Christianity) "Minister (Christianity)") who refused to accept these changes were expelled, leading to a divide recognised by the [Scottish Episcopalians Act 1711](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopalians_Act_1711 "Scottish Episcopalians Act 1711"), which created a separate [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"). When [George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") became king in 1714, most Scottish Episcopalians refused to swear allegiance to the [Hanoverian](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") regime, creating a split that lasted until the death of [Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") in 1788.

The Non-Juring movement in the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") was insignificant, although it produced the [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") propagandist [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Leslie_(nonjuror) "Charles Leslie (nonjuror)"). The Episcopal church in North America was then part of the Church of England, but largely unaffected until after the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") when the Scottish non-juror liturgy influenced that of the new [U.S. Episcopal Church](/wiki/U.S._Episcopal_Church "U.S. Episcopal Church").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/AcquittalSevenBishops.jpg/250px-AcquittalSevenBishops.jpg)](/wiki/File:AcquittalSevenBishops.jpg)

The [Seven Bishops](/wiki/Seven_Bishops "Seven Bishops"), June 1688; their acquittal was a key factor in the removal of King [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), but five became Non-Jurors out of conscience

In modern usage, [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") and [Episcopalian](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") implies differences in both governance and doctrine but this was not the case in the 17th and 18th centuries. *Episcopalian* structures were governed by bishops, appointed by the monarch; *Presbyterian* implied rule by [Elders](/wiki/Elder_(Christianity) "Elder (Christianity)"), nominated by congregations. In an era when "true religion" and "true government" were assumed to be the same thing, arguments over church governance and practice often reflected political differences, not simply religious ones.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood195593–94-1)

In 1688, all three [established churches](/wiki/Established_church "Established church") were Episcopalian in structure and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") in doctrine, but faced different challenges. In England, over 90% belonged to the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), while the majority of those excluded were [Protestant Nonconformists](/wiki/Protestant_Nonconformists "Protestant Nonconformists") who wanted to reverse the [Act of Uniformity 1662](/wiki/Act_of_Uniformity_1662 "Act of Uniformity 1662") and be allowed to rejoin the Church.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978171–172-2) In Ireland, over 75% of the population were Catholic, while the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") was a minority even among Irish Protestants, the majority of whom were Nonconformists concentrated in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"). Nearly 98% of Scots were members of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), or kirk, far closer in doctrine to Irish Nonconformists than the Church of England and an organisational hybrid, with bishops presiding over Presbyterian structures.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcDonald199875–76-3)

James became king in 1685 with widespread support in all three kingdoms but this changed when his policies seemed to move beyond tolerance for Catholicism and into an attack on the established church. The 1638 to 1652 [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms") highlighted the dangers of religious division and moderates on both sides wanted to avoid a [schism](/wiki/Schism "Schism"). Many supported James in 1685 from fear of civil war if he were bypassed; by 1688, it seemed only his deposition could prevent one.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWormsley2015189-4) His prosecution of the [Seven Bishops](/wiki/Seven_Bishops "Seven Bishops") for seditious libel in June 1688 alienated the [Tories](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") who formed his main English support base. Many viewed this as a violation of his coronation oath promising to maintain the primacy of the Church of England, making compliance with oaths a key issue.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2007179–181-5)

## English Non-Juring movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: English Non-Juring movement")]

"Nonjuror" generally means those who refused to take the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oaths_of_Allegiance_and_Supremacy_Act_1688 "Oaths of Allegiance and Supremacy Act 1688") to the new monarchs, [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II"). It includes "Non-Abjurors", those who refused to swear the [Oath of Abjuration](/wiki/Security_of_the_Succession,_etc._Act_1701 "Security of the Succession, etc. Act 1701") in 1701 and 1714, requiring them to deny the Stuart claim.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOllard1912412-6) Nine bishops became nonjurors, including [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") [William Sancroft](/wiki/William_Sancroft "William Sancroft") and five of the seven bishops prosecuted by James in June 1688.[[a]](#cite_note-7) One study estimates that 339 members of the clergy became nonjurors, around 2% of the total; of these, 80 subsequently conformed, offset by another 130 who refused the Oath of Abjuration in either 1701 or 1714. This ignores natural decline, so the actual number at any time would have been lower, while the majority were concentrated in areas like [London](/wiki/London "London") and [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), implying large parts of England were untouched by the controversy.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalsh199331-8)

Identifying lay members is more complex, since only those holding a public office were required to swear. One source identifies a total of 584 clergy, schoolmasters and university dons as Non Jurors, but this almost certainly understates their numbers.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOverton1902467–496-9) The reasons for non-compliance varied; some, like Bishop [Thomas Ken](/wiki/Thomas_Ken "Thomas Ken"), considered themselves bound by their oath to James, but did not oppose the new government and continued to attend church services. Others argued the new regime was illegitimate, since divine right and inheritance meant kings could not be removed, the so-called "state point". A more fundamental issue was the 'church point', the belief Parliament had no right to intervene in ecclesiastical affairs, whether appointing or removing bishops and clergy, or changes to church policies.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHastings1917395-10)

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/George_Hickes.jpg/250px-George_Hickes.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Hickes.jpg)

Non Juror bishop [George Hickes](/wiki/George_Hickes_(divine) "George Hickes (divine)") (1642–1715), the driving force behind the schismatic Non Juror church

Regular clergy were largely undisturbed and although the nonjuring bishops were suspended in February 1690, William kept them in office as he tried to negotiate a compromise. When this failed, in May 1691 [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") appointed six new bishops, three of the original nine having since died. In May 1692, Sancroft delegated his powers to [Bishop Lloyd](/wiki/William_Lloyd_(bishop_of_Norwich) "William Lloyd (bishop of Norwich)"), who in May 1693 appointed [Thomas Wagstaffe](/wiki/Thomas_Wagstaffe "Thomas Wagstaffe") (1645–1712) and [George Hickes](/wiki/George_Hickes_(divine) "George Hickes (divine)") (1642–1715) as new Non-Juring bishops. Lloyd argued he simply wanted to establish the principle Parliament had no right to deprive or appoint bishops, but it created a formal schism; the vast majority remained within the established church, while Wagstaffe refused to exercise his powers.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell1937205-11)

Some prominent nonjurors returned to the church in 1710, including [Henry Dodwell](/wiki/Henry_Dodwell "Henry Dodwell") (1641–1711), and [Robert Nelson](/wiki/Robert_Nelson_(nonjuror) "Robert Nelson (nonjuror)") (1656–1715). Hickes, whose hardline views on divine right and the primacy of Stuart authority led to his appointment as [Charles II's](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") chaplain in 1683, was the main driver behind the nonjuror church; it sharply declined after his death in 1715.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarmsen2008-12) The death of Thomas Wagstaffe in October 1712 left Hickes as the last surviving Non-Juring bishop. To ensure the survival of the church, Hickes and two bishops from the [Scottish Episcopalian Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopalian_Church "Scottish Episcopalian Church"), Archibald Campbell and James Gadderar, consecrated [Jeremy Collier](/wiki/Jeremy_Collier "Jeremy Collier"), [Nathaniel Spinckes](/wiki/Nathaniel_Spinckes "Nathaniel Spinckes") and [Samuel Hawes](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Hawes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samuel Hawes (page does not exist)") as bishops in June 1713.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOverton1902150-13)

In 1719, the nonjuror church split into "Usager" and "Non-Usager" factions, with both sides consecrating their own bishops. Effectively, Non-Usagers wanted an eventual reconciliation with the main Church of England, while Usagers wanted to restore traditional liturgies, including use of the [1549 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1549) "Book of Common Prayer (1549)").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell1937207-14) In 1716, the Usagers initiated discussions with the [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") on union, which continued until 1725 before both sides admitted failure.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShukman2006175–191-15) Despite the two factions agreeing to re-unite in 1732, divisions continued with a Usager contingent led by Bishop Archibald Campbell and a small London-based Non-Usager grouping under Bishop John Blackbourne.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2013-16)

By the early 1740s, significant Non-Juring congregations were restricted to Newcastle, London and [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYates201718-17) In 1741, Robert Gordon became the last regular Non-Juring bishop, his consecration being agreed by the *de jure* James III; he died in 1779 and his congregation in London was absorbed by the Scottish Episcopal church. This left a small congregation in [Holborn](/wiki/Holborn "Holborn"), which was strongly Jacobite and in 1788 refused to acknowledge George III, before disappearing in the 1790s. Bishop Campbell was succeeded by the Usager [Thomas Deacon](/wiki/Thomas_Deacon "Thomas Deacon"), who led a separate group in Manchester known as the Orthodox British Church, or "OBC". It strayed further away from the Church of England, investigating primitive liturgies and insisting on no State control and was Jacobite in sympathy; several members, including three of Deacon's sons, joined the [Manchester Regiment](/wiki/Manchester_Regiment_(Jacobite) "Manchester Regiment (Jacobite)") that participated in the [1745 Rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2021?-18) The OBC limped on into the early nineteenth century, but was wound up within a decade after the death of Bishop William Cartwright of Shrewsbury in 1799. There are suggestions elements of its theology resurfaced in the 1830s [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell1937211-19)

### Numbers and significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Numbers and significance")]

Membership of the schismatic church was initially confined to clerics, then later expanded into the lay population. Largely restricted to urban areas, its congregations continually shifted, making it difficult to assess numbers; it is suggested these were negligible, certainly fewer than Catholics, who were around 1% of the population.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi200654-20) Many more were so-called 'crypto-Non Jurors', those who remained with the established church after 1693, but shared some Non Juror concerns; while sympathetic to the Stuarts, only a few were active [Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism"). Typical of this [High Church](/wiki/High_Church "High Church"), [Tory](/wiki/Tory "Tory") group was [Lady Elizabeth Hastings](/wiki/Lady_Elizabeth_Hastings "Lady Elizabeth Hastings"), daughter of the [Earl of Huntingdon](/wiki/Theophilus_Hastings,_7th_Earl_of_Huntingdon "Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon"), a Stuart loyalist. Their support was based on the 'church point', and they opposed measures seen as diminishing the primacy of the Church of England. These included the [1689 Toleration Act](/wiki/Toleration_Act_1689 "Toleration Act 1689"), and '[occasional conformity](/wiki/Occasional_conformity "Occasional conformity")', allowing Catholics and [Nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") limited freedom of worship.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaningam197739–41-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Lady_Betty.jpg/250px-Lady_Betty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Betty.jpg)

[Lady Elizabeth Hastings](/wiki/Lady_Elizabeth_Hastings "Lady Elizabeth Hastings"), typical of the High Church Tories associated with the Non Jurors, whose influence was far greater than their numbers

As a result, Stuart Catholicism was an insuperable barrier to their restoration, although attempts were made to convert James and his successors; when [Prince Charles](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") visited [London](/wiki/London "London") in 1750, he was inducted into the Non-Juring church, probably by Bishop Robert Gordon.[[b]](#cite_note-22)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2013-16) Despite their limited numbers, Non Juror clergy exercised significant influence over church policy. Many opposed post 1689 changes that moved the Church of England away from [Laudian](/wiki/Laudian "Laudian") principles of authority, and allowed greater tolerance of different practices.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeza197363–65-23) This led to demands for [Convocation](/wiki/Convocations_of_Canterbury_and_York "Convocations of Canterbury and York"), or church assembly, to have a greater say over policy; it is suggested this was a contest between a largely Tory, High Church clerical body, and [Whig](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") bishops. [Francis Atterbury](/wiki/Francis_Atterbury "Francis Atterbury"), later associated with a [1722 Jacobite plot](/wiki/Atterbury_Plot "Atterbury Plot"), was a prominent supporter of Convocation, although not a Non Juror himself.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeza197368-24)

Outside politics, the Non Juror movement had an impact far greater than often appreciated, which continues today. Lady Elizabeth was part of a network of wealthy High Church philanthropists, linked by Non-Jurors like Robert Nelson, who supported measures intended to eliminate 'un-Christian behaviour', such as the conversion of Catholics and Nonconformists. In 1698, they established the [Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge](/wiki/Society_for_Promoting_Christian_Knowledge "Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge"), or SPCK, followed in 1701 by the [Society for the Propagation of the Gospel in Foreign Parts](/wiki/United_Society_Partners_in_the_Gospel "United Society Partners in the Gospel"), or SPG, which are still in existence. Its members included academics like [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith_(academic) "Joseph Smith (academic)"), who was a close advisor to Lady Elizabeth, and was well known for converting English Catholics.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaurence2010203-25) In 1701, the SPG sent [George Keith](/wiki/George_Keith_(missionary) "George Keith (missionary)") to [New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey"), charged with converting [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuncan1973239-26)

Some later became early advocates of [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), which began as a reform movement within the Church of England. One of these was Lady Elizabeth Hastings' daughter-in-law, [Selina Hastings](/wiki/Selina_Hastings,_Countess_of_Huntingdon "Selina Hastings, Countess of Huntingdon") (1707–1791), founder of the [evangelical](/wiki/Christian_revival "Christian revival") Methodist sect known as the [Countess of Huntingdon's Connexion](/wiki/Countess_of_Huntingdon%27s_Connexion "Countess of Huntingdon's Connexion").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlenther2008-27) Methodists were often accused of Jacobitism, because they rejected existing structures and practices; conversely, many "Jacobite" demonstrations during the 1730s were led by Tories hostile to the [Welsh Methodist revival](/wiki/Welsh_Methodist_revival "Welsh Methodist revival").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod1993197–199-28) Although associated with [social conservatism](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism"), crypto Non-Jurors included [Mary Astell](/wiki/Mary_Astell "Mary Astell") (1666–1731), an educationalist sometimes called the first English feminist.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELivingstone199887-29) Daughter of a wealthy, upper-class Non-Juror merchant, in 1709, she set up a girls' school in [Chelsea, London](/wiki/Chelsea,_London "Chelsea, London"). Supported by the SPCK, Nelson, Dodwell, Lady Elizabeth and [Lady Catherine Jones](/wiki/Lady_Catherine_Jones "Lady Catherine Jones"), it is thought to be the first in England with an all-women Board of Governors.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScott1983100-30)

## Irish Non-Juring movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Irish Non-Juring movement")]

The [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") was a minority, even among Irish Protestants, and during the 1689 [Parliament](/wiki/Patriot_Parliament "Patriot Parliament") called by James, four bishops sat in the [Lords](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Lords "Irish House of Lords"), with [Anthony Dopping, Bishop of Meath](/wiki/Anthony_Dopping "Anthony Dopping") acting as leader of the opposition.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoodyMartinByrne2009489-31) Non Juror Henry Dodwell was born and educated in Ireland, but spent his career in England, making [William Sheridan, Bishop of Kilmore and Ardagh](/wiki/William_Sheridan_(Bishop_of_Kilmore_and_Ardagh) "William Sheridan (Bishop of Kilmore and Ardagh)") the most significant Irish Non Juror. Son of a Catholic convert, he lost office as a result, and later died in poverty in London. Only a handful of Irish clergy followed his example, the most notable being [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") propagandist [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Leslie_(nonjuror) "Charles Leslie (nonjuror)").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiggins201478-32)

As in England, many sympathised with aspects of Non Juror policy; [John Pooley, Bishop of Raphoe](/wiki/John_Pooley "John Pooley"), avoided taking the Oath of Abjuration until 1710, while [Bishop Palliser](/wiki/William_Palliser_(bishop) "William Palliser (bishop)") was a long time correspondent of Dodwell and Non Juror historian [Thomas Smith. Bishop Lindsay](/wiki/Thomas_Lindsay_(bishop) "Thomas Lindsay (bishop)"), later [Archbishop of Armagh](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Armagh_(Church_of_Ireland) "Archbishop of Armagh (Church of Ireland)"), was a close friend of [Jacobite plotter](/wiki/Atterbury_Plot "Atterbury Plot") Francis Atterbury, and himself accused of Jacobitism in 1714. However, most of these links appear to have been driven by friendship, rather than political belief.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle199732–34-33)

## Scottish Non Jurors and the Scottish Episcopal Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Scottish Non Jurors and the Scottish Episcopal Church")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Charles_Edward_Stuart_%281775%29.jpg/250px-Charles_Edward_Stuart_%281775%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Edward_Stuart_(1775).jpg)

[Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart"); after his death in 1788, the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church") ended the schism by taking the Oath to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III")

English Non Juring was largely a split *within* Episcopalianism, but this was not the case in Scotland, where the religious conflicts of the 17th century normalised the eviction of defeated opponents. In 1688, Scots were divided roughly equally into Presbyterians and Episcopalians, the latter concentrated in the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"), [Banffshire](/wiki/Banffshire "Banffshire"), [Perthshire](/wiki/Perthshire "Perthshire"), [Angus](/wiki/Angus,_Scotland "Angus, Scotland") and [Aberdeenshire](/wiki/Aberdeenshire_(historic) "Aberdeenshire (historic)"). The 1690 [General Assembly of the Church of Scotland](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_the_Church_of_Scotland "General Assembly of the Church of Scotland") abolished bishops and expelled 200 ministers who refused to accept these changes.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch1992300-34)

As in England, many remained in place; Michael Fraser served as minister of Daviot and Dunlichty continuously from 1673 to 1726, despite being evicted in 1694 and joining both the [1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") and [1719](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1719 "Jacobite rising of 1719") Risings.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenman198056-35) Moderates within the kirk facilitated the readmission of deprived ministers; from 1690 to 1693, 70 of the 200 returned after taking the Oath of Allegiance, plus another 116 after the 1695 Act of Toleration. In 1702, a contemporary estimated 200 of 796 parishes were held by Episcopalian ministers, but the numbers above suggest many were not Non Jurors.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrace2008361-36)

Before 1690, differences were largely about governance, but as they dwindled in numbers, Scottish Episcopalians increasingly focused on doctrine. They saw the [1707 Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") as an opportunity to regain power via a unified British church, and began using English liturgy to help this process.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephen2013294-37) The [Scottish Episcopalians Act 1711](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopalians_Act_1711 "Scottish Episcopalians Act 1711") ([10 Ann.](/wiki/10_Ann. "10 Ann.") c. 10) gave a legal basis for the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"), while the [Toleration Act 1711](/wiki/Toleration_Act_1711 "Toleration Act 1711") ([10 Ann.](/wiki/10_Ann. "10 Ann.") c. 6) provided legal protection for use of the [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer"), whose rejection in 1637 had sparked the [Bishops' Wars](/wiki/Bishops%27_Wars "Bishops' Wars").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickinson2006268-38)

When [George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") succeeded [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") in 1714, the church split into a majority Non-Juror element and [Qualified Chapels](/wiki/Qualified_Chapel "Qualified Chapel"), those willing to swear allegiance to the Hanoverian regime.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertie2000649-39) Non-Juring Episcopalianism became a mark of Jacobite commitment and a high percentage of both Lowlanders and Highlanders who participated in the 1745 Rising came from this element of Scottish society.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechiSankey200197-40)

Post 1745, many Non-Juror meeting houses were closed or destroyed, and further restrictions placed on their clergy and congregants. When Prince Charles died in 1788, he was succeeded by his brother [Henry](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart"), a [Catholic cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)"), and the Episcopal Church now swore allegiance to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), ending the schism with the Qualified Chapels, although the Qualified Chapel in [Montrose](/wiki/Montrose,_Angus "Montrose, Angus") remained independent until 1920.
[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertie2000649-39) In 1788 Bishop Charles Rose of Dunblane, and one presbyter, James Brown of Montrose, refused to acknowledge George III and his family, forming a breakaway nonjuring church based in Edinburgh that acknowledged Henry Stuart. However, the church finally ended in 1808 with the death of their last clergyman [Donald Macintosh](/wiki/Donald_Macintosh "Donald Macintosh"), a noted Gaelic scholar. When the penal laws were finally lifted in 1792, the Church had fewer than 15,000 members, less than one percent of the Scottish population.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickinson2006268-38) Today, the Scottish Episcopal Church is sometimes [pejoratively](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative") referred to as the "English Kirk".[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)

## Non Jurors in North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non Jurors in North America")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/William_White-Bishop_Episcopal_Church_USA-1795.jpg/250px-William_White-Bishop_Episcopal_Church_USA-1795.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_White-Bishop_Episcopal_Church_USA-1795.jpg)

[William White](/wiki/William_White_(bishop_of_Pennsylvania) "William White (bishop of Pennsylvania)"), appointed Episcopalian bishop of Pennsylvania in 1787

The political conflicts of the 1688 Glorious Revolution were reflected to a lesser degree in [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") and the Caribbean. The proximity of Catholic [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") meant limited sympathy for James; in 1700, [New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") banned Catholic priests from the state. However, [Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia") removed several Non Jurors from office in 1691.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParrish201347-43)

[Richard Welton](/wiki/Richard_Welton "Richard Welton") became a Non Juror in 1714, losing his parish in [Whitechapel](/wiki/Whitechapel "Whitechapel") as a result; in 1724, he was ordained a bishop by Non-Usager [Ralph Taylor](/wiki/Ralph_Taylor_(divine) "Ralph Taylor (divine)"), and moved to North America. He replaced John Urmiston as [Rector](/wiki/Rector_(ecclesiastical) "Rector (ecclesiastical)") of [Christ Church, Philadelphia](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Philadelphia "Christ Church, Philadelphia"); Urmiston demanded he be reinstated, and the dispute drew in John Talbot, rector of [St. Mary's Episcopal Church, Burlington, New Jersey](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Episcopal_Church,_Burlington,_New_Jersey "St. Mary's Episcopal Church, Burlington, New Jersey") since April 1704.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuncan1973241-44)

Episcopalian ministers were in short supply in North America, one reason being delays caused by the need for London to approve appointments; between 1712 and 1720, Talbot presented numerous petitions requesting the appointment of a bishop.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuncan1973250–252-45) On a visit to England in 1722, Taylor made him a Non Juror bishop, and when Pennsylvania Governor Sir [William Keith](/wiki/Sir_William_Keith,_4th_Baronet "Sir William Keith, 4th Baronet") informed the church authorities, Talbot and Welton were suspended. Talbot remained in North America, where he died in 1727; Welton returned to England in 1726, dying shortly afterwards in [Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwall2004-46)

Failure to establish a colonial episcopate was partly due to opposition by American Nonconformists; combined with the activities of the SPG, they viewed it as an attempt to impose a state church. In 1815, [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") claimed a key element in mobilising popular support for the 1773 [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") was the "apprehension of Episcopacy".[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalker2017306–308-47)

After the outbreak of the Revolution, many Episcopal ministers remained [loyal to the London government](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)"), and were removed. In Pennsylvania, most supported the [Patriots](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)"), including [William White](/wiki/William_White_(bishop_of_Pennsylvania) "William White (bishop of Pennsylvania)"), who set out principles establishing the [American Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"). These were accepted by all the states, apart from [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), which insisted each state must be controlled by its own bishop.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMills2004-48)

Connecticut sent Loyalist [Samuel Seabury](/wiki/Samuel_Seabury_(bishop) "Samuel Seabury (bishop)") to England to be made a bishop, but since he could not take the required Oath of Allegiance to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), he was consecrated instead by the Scottish church in November 1784. Concerned at the prospect of another schism, Parliament agreed to waive the oath, and on 4 February 1787, White was consecrated [bishop of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bishop_of_Pennsylvania "Bishop of Pennsylvania") by the archbishop of Canterbury, [John Moore](/wiki/John_Moore_(Archbishop_of_Canterbury) "John Moore (Archbishop of Canterbury)").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMills2004-48)

## Prominent Nonjurors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nonjuring_schism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Prominent Nonjurors")]

* [Hilkiah Bedford](/wiki/Hilkiah_Bedford "Hilkiah Bedford") (1663–1724), chaplain to Bishop Ken, author of *Vindication of the Church of England* (1710)
* John Blackbourne (1681–1741), Non-Usager Bishop
* Charles Booth (d. c. 1810), last consecrated irregular nonjuring bishop
* [William Bowyer](/wiki/William_Bowyer_(printer) "William Bowyer (printer)") the Younger (1699–1777), "the learned printer"
* [Thomas Brett](/wiki/Thomas_Brett_(nonjuror) "Thomas Brett (nonjuror)") (1667–1743), of Spring Garden, liturgical scholar, Nonjuring bishop
* [Archibald Campbell](/wiki/Archibald_Campbell_(bishop) "Archibald Campbell (bishop)") (d. 1744), Scottish bishop, usager bishop, author of *The Middle State*
* William Cartwright (1730–99), Nonjuring Bishop in [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury")
* [Jeremy Collier](/wiki/Jeremy_Collier "Jeremy Collier") (1650–1726), Nonjuring primus, ecclesiastical historian, writer, and critic
* [Thomas Deacon](/wiki/Thomas_Deacon "Thomas Deacon") (1697–1753), stepson of Jeremy Collier, liturgical scholar, bishop of Manchester;
* [Henry Dodwell](/wiki/Henry_Dodwell "Henry Dodwell") (1641–1711), Anglo-Irish patristic scholar, Oxford don, author of *The Case in View*
* [Elijah Fenton](/wiki/Elijah_Fenton "Elijah Fenton") (1683–1730), poet
* [Robert Forbes](/wiki/Robert_Forbes_(bishop) "Robert Forbes (bishop)") (1708–1775), Bishop of Ross and [Caithness](/wiki/Caithness "Caithness") and historian of the [Jacobite Rising of 1745](/wiki/Jacobite_Rising_of_1745 "Jacobite Rising of 1745")
* [Marmaduke Fothergill](/wiki/Marmaduke_Fothergill "Marmaduke Fothergill") (1652–1731), Vicar of [Skipwith](/wiki/Skipwith "Skipwith") and antiquarian
* [Henry Gandy](/wiki/Henry_Gandy "Henry Gandy") (1649–1734), bishop in Non-Usager succession
* Thomas Garnett (d. 1818), of Manchester, bishop in irregular usager line
* Robert Gordon (1703–1779), last bishop in the regular Nonjuring succession. Also called Gordoun.
* [Lady Elizabeth Hastings](/wiki/Lady_Elizabeth_Hastings "Lady Elizabeth Hastings") (1682–1739); philanthropist, early supporter of [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism")
* [Thomas Hearne](/wiki/Thomas_Hearne_(antiquarian) "Thomas Hearne (antiquarian)") (1678–1735), antiquarian and diarist
* [Charles Jennens](/wiki/Charles_Jennens "Charles Jennens") (1700–73), librettist for [Handel's *Messiah*](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)"), [S*aul*](/wiki/Saul_(Handel) "Saul (Handel)"), *[Belshazzar](/wiki/Belshazzar_(Handel) "Belshazzar (Handel)"),* and other oratorios
* [William Law](/wiki/William_Law "William Law") (1686–1761)
* [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Leslie_(nonjuror) "Charles Leslie (nonjuror)") (1650–1721), Jacobite propagandist, author of *The Rehearsal* and *Short and Easy Method with the Deists*
* [Robert Nelson](/wiki/Robert_Nelson_(nonjuror) "Robert Nelson (nonjuror)") (1656–1715), founding member of the [Society for the Propagation of Christian Knowledge](/wiki/Society_for_the_Propagation_of_Christian_Knowledge "Society for the Propagation of Christian Knowledge");
* [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys") (1633–1703), Secretary to the Admiralty under Charles II and James II, diarist.
* Thomas Podmore (1705–84), nonjuring Deacon in Shrewsbury. Author of 'The Layman's Apology etc.'
* [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith_(academic) "Joseph Smith (academic)") (1670–1756), Provost of [The Queen's College, Oxford](/wiki/The_Queen%27s_College,_Oxford "The Queen's College, Oxford")
* Thomas Smith (1638–1710), historian and librarian
* [Richard Rawlinson](/wiki/Richard_Rawlinson "Richard Rawlinson") (1690–1755) clergyman, book collector, antiquarian, and nonjuring bishop
* [Ralph Taylor](/wiki/Ralph_Taylor_(divine) "Ralph Taylor (divine)") (1647–1722), nonjuring bishop in Non-Usager line
* [John Urry](/wiki/John_Urry_(literary_editor) "John Urry (literary editor)") (1666–1715), literary scholar, editor of [Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")
* [Thomas Wagstaffe the Elder](/wiki/Thomas_Wagstaffe_the_Elder "Thomas Wagstaffe the Elder") (1645–1712), nonjuring bishop and vindicator of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England")
* Thomas Wagstaffe the Younger (1692–1770), usager controversialist, later Anglican Chaplain at the Stuart court in Rome
